title: Testing
description: Unit tests, UI tests, testing coroutines, screenshot testing, and the testing pyramid
lesson:
The Testing Pyramid

Android testing follows a pyramid model: many fast unit tests at the base, fewer integration tests in the middle, and the fewest UI/E2E tests at the top. This shape reflects the cost-benefit tradeoffs at each level. Google explicitly recommends this distribution in their official Android testing documentation.

/ UI / E2E \ ~10% — Slowest, flakiest, highest confidence / \ Tests real user journeys end-to-end / Integration \ ~20% — Medium speed, real components / \ Tests component interactions / Unit Tests \ ~70% — Fastest, most reliable / \ Tests single class in isolation ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Unit Tests (~70% of your suite)

Unit tests verify a single class or function in complete isolation. Dependencies are replaced with test doubles (fakes, stubs). They run on the JVM in milliseconds, require no Android device or emulator, and provide extremely fast feedback during development. Examples: testing a ViewModel's state transitions, a Repository's data mapping logic, a utility function's edge cases, or a use case's business rules.

Integration Tests (~20% of your suite)

Integration tests verify that two or more real components work correctly together. They catch bugs that unit tests miss -- mismatched data formats between layers, incorrect SQL queries against a real Room database, serialization issues with a real Retrofit call. They are slower (seconds) but provide higher confidence that the system works as a whole. Examples: Repository + Room DAO using an in-memory database, API client + MockWebServer, ViewModel + real repository with fake data source.

UI / E2E Tests (~10% of your suite)

End-to-end tests verify complete user journeys through the actual UI. They launch Activities or Composables on a device/emulator and simulate real user interactions (taps, swipes, text input). They are the slowest (minutes), most flaky (sensitive to animations, timing, device state), but provide the highest confidence that the app works from the user's perspective. Reserve them for critical flows: login, checkout, onboarding.

Cost / Speed / Confidence Tradeoffs: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Speed Cost Confidence Flakiness Where ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Unit ~ms Low Low-Medium Very Low test/ Integration ~sec Medium Medium-High Low test/ or androidTest/ E2E / UI ~min High Highest High androidTest/ ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Rule: Write MANY fast unit tests, FEW slow UI tests. Unit tests catch most bugs. UI tests catch integration issues. Integration tests fill the gap in between.
Key insight: Unit tests are cheap and fast -- write many. UI tests are expensive and flaky -- write few but cover critical user journeys. Integration tests fill the gap, testing real component interactions without full UI. A healthy test suite runs in seconds, not minutes.
Anti-pattern: The Ice Cream Cone. Some teams invert the pyramid -- mostly manual/UI tests, few unit tests. This leads to slow CI, flaky builds, and fear of refactoring. If your test suite takes 30+ minutes and fails randomly, you probably have an inverted pyramid. Fix it by pushing test coverage down to the unit level.

Test Doubles: Fake, Mock, Stub, Spy

Test doubles replace real dependencies in tests. Each type serves a different purpose, and choosing the right one matters for test quality, readability, and resilience to refactoring.

Test Double Types: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ FAKE Working implementation with shortcuts (in-memory, simplified) Example: FakeUserRepository backed by HashMap When: Most tests. Google's recommended default. Why: Tests BEHAVIOR, not implementation. Survives refactoring. STUB Returns hardcoded/preconfigured values, no real logic Example: always returns User("Alice") regardless of input When: You only care about the return value, not behavior. Why: Simplest possible double. Good for simple dependency outputs. MOCK Records interactions for verification after the fact Example: verify(repo).save(user) was called exactly once When: You MUST verify a method was called (e.g., analytics event sent). Library: Mockito (Java), MockK (Kotlin) Why: Only use when behavior IS the call itself (fire-and-forget). SPY Wraps a real object, lets you override specific methods Example: real repo but intercept network calls When: You need mostly real behavior with one controlled override. Why: Last resort. Hard to reason about -- partial real, partial fake. ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Preference order: Fake > Stub > Mock > Spy Fakes test WHAT code does. Mocks test HOW code does it. Fakes survive refactoring. Mocks break when internals change.
// FAKE -- has real logic, uses in-memory data class FakeUserRepository : UserRepository { private val users = mutableMapOf<String, User>() var shouldThrow = false // configurable error simulation var networkDelay = 0L // configurable latency override suspend fun getUser(id: String): User? { if (shouldThrow) throw IOException("Fake network error") if (networkDelay > 0) delay(networkDelay) return users[id] } override suspend fun saveUser(user: User) { if (shouldThrow) throw IOException("Fake network error") users[user.id] = user } override suspend fun deleteUser(id: String) { users.remove(id) } override suspend fun getAllUsers(): List<User> = users.values.toList() // Test helpers -- make setup easy fun seedUsers(vararg userList: User) { userList.forEach { users[it.id] = it } } fun clear() { users.clear() } }
// STUB -- returns preconfigured values, no internal logic class StubTimeProvider : TimeProvider { var now: Long = 1_000_000L override fun currentTimeMillis(): Long = now } // MOCK -- using MockK to verify interactions @Test fun saveUser_callsAnalytics() = runTest { val analytics = mockk<Analytics>(relaxed = true) val repo = UserRepository(FakeDb(), analytics) repo.saveUser(User("1", "Alice")) // Verify the analytics event was fired verify(exactly = 1) { analytics.logEvent("user_saved", any()) } } // SPY -- wrap real object, override one method @Test fun cacheFallback_onNetworkError() = runTest { val realRepo = UserRepositoryImpl(db, api) val spyRepo = spyk(realRepo) every { spyRepo.fetchFromNetwork(any()) } throws IOException() val result = spyRepo.getUser("1") // falls back to cache assertNotNull(result) }
Why Google recommends fakes over mocks: Mocks verify HOW code works (implementation details) -- refactoring breaks tests even if behavior is correct. If you rename an internal method, mock-based tests break. Fakes verify WHAT code does (behavior) -- tests survive refactoring because they only assert on outputs. Mocks also make tests harder to read: every { } returns { } verify { } is harder to follow than fakeRepo.seedUsers(alice); assertEquals(alice, viewModel.user.value). Use mocks only when the side effect IS the behavior (e.g., verifying an analytics event was sent).
Decision guide: Ask yourself: "Am I testing what the code DOES, or how it does it?" If you're testing outputs and state changes, use a Fake. If you're testing that a specific method was called (fire-and-forget side effect), use a Mock. If you need a simple return value, use a Stub. If you need 95% real behavior with one tweak, consider a Spy -- but usually a Fake is better.

ViewModel Testing

ViewModels are the most frequently tested component in Android apps. They contain business logic, manage UI state, and interact with repositories via coroutines. Testing them requires three key pieces: a TestDispatcher to replace Dispatchers.Main, runTest to handle coroutines, and Turbine to assert on Flow/StateFlow emissions.

The Essential ViewModel Test Setup

class UserViewModelTest { // Replace Dispatchers.Main (doesn't exist in JVM tests!) // UnconfinedTestDispatcher runs coroutines eagerly -- good for simple tests private val testDispatcher = UnconfinedTestDispatcher() @Before fun setup() { // viewModelScope uses Dispatchers.Main internally // This replaces it with our test dispatcher Dispatchers.setMain(testDispatcher) } @After fun tearDown() { // ALWAYS reset to avoid leaking into other tests Dispatchers.resetMain() } }
Why setMain is required: ViewModels use viewModelScope which internally dispatches on Dispatchers.Main. In JVM unit tests, there is no Main dispatcher (it's an Android UI thread concept). Without Dispatchers.setMain(), your test will crash with: Module with the Main dispatcher had failed to initialize.

Testing State Transitions with Turbine

// Turbine lets you assert on individual StateFlow emissions in order @Test fun loadUsers_emitsLoadingThenSuccess() = runTest { val fakeRepo = FakeUserRepository() fakeRepo.seedUsers(User("1", "Alice"), User("2", "Bob")) val viewModel = UserViewModel(fakeRepo) // .test { } collects emissions and lets you assert them one by one viewModel.uiState.test { // First emission: initial/loading state assertEquals(UiState.Loading, awaitItem()) viewModel.loadUsers() // Second emission: success with data val success = awaitItem() assertTrue(success is UiState.Success) assertEquals(2, (success as UiState.Success).users.size) // Clean up: cancel collection and consume any remaining events cancelAndConsumeRemainingEvents() } }

Testing Error States

@Test fun loadUsers_emitsErrorOnFailure() = runTest { val fakeRepo = FakeUserRepository().apply { shouldThrow = true // configure fake to simulate network error } val viewModel = UserViewModel(fakeRepo) viewModel.loadUsers() // Assert the ViewModel caught the exception and mapped it to Error state val state = viewModel.uiState.value assertTrue(state is UiState.Error) assertEquals("Fake network error", (state as UiState.Error).message) } // Testing retry logic @Test fun retry_afterError_loadsSuccessfully() = runTest { val fakeRepo = FakeUserRepository() fakeRepo.shouldThrow = true val viewModel = UserViewModel(fakeRepo) viewModel.loadUsers() assertTrue(viewModel.uiState.value is UiState.Error) // Fix the error condition, then retry fakeRepo.shouldThrow = false fakeRepo.seedUsers(User("1", "Alice")) viewModel.retry() assertTrue(viewModel.uiState.value is UiState.Success) }

Testing User Actions and Events

// Testing one-shot events (navigation, snackbar) via Channel/SharedFlow @Test fun deleteUser_emitsNavigateBackEvent() = runTest { val viewModel = UserViewModel(FakeUserRepository()) viewModel.events.test { viewModel.deleteUser("1") val event = awaitItem() assertEquals(UserEvent.NavigateBack, event) cancelAndConsumeRemainingEvents() } } // Testing SavedStateHandle in ViewModel @Test fun viewModel_restoresStateFromSavedState() = runTest { val savedState = SavedStateHandle(mapOf("userId" to "42")) val viewModel = UserViewModel(FakeUserRepository(), savedState) // ViewModel should load user "42" from the saved state viewModel.uiState.test { val state = awaitItem() assertTrue(state is UiState.Success) assertEquals("42", (state as UiState.Success).user.id) cancelAndConsumeRemainingEvents() } }

Coroutine Testing Deep Dive

The kotlinx-coroutines-test library provides tools to test coroutines deterministically. The key concept is virtual time: delays don't actually wait, and you control when coroutines execute.

runTest: The Foundation

// runTest: the coroutine test harness // - Creates a TestScope with a TestCoroutineScheduler // - Automatically advances time for delays // - Fails if coroutines leak (unfinished child coroutines) @Test fun testWithDelay() = runTest { var result = 0 launch { delay(1_000) // virtual time -- doesn't actually wait! result = 42 } advanceTimeBy(1_000) // fast-forward virtual clock by 1 second assertEquals(42, result) } // runTest auto-advances idle time at the end @Test fun autoAdvance() = runTest { var result = 0 launch { delay(5_000) result = 42 } // Even without advanceTimeBy, runTest auto-completes // all pending coroutines before the test ends advanceUntilIdle() assertEquals(42, result) }

StandardTestDispatcher vs UnconfinedTestDispatcher

StandardTestDispatcher vs UnconfinedTestDispatcher: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ StandardTestDispatcher UnconfinedTestDispatcher ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Coroutines DON'T run Coroutines run EAGERLY automatically. They are queued. (immediately on launch, no queue) You control execution: Simpler for most tests. advanceUntilIdle() - run all No need to call advance. advanceTimeBy(ms) - time travel Coroutines just execute. runCurrent() - run ready ones Best for: testing timing, Best for: ViewModel tests delays, debounce, throttle, where you just want to timeout, precise ordering, verify final state quickly. retry-with-backoff logic. Default in runTest { }. Must be explicitly created. ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Tip: Use Standard when time matters, Unconfined when it doesn't.
// StandardTestDispatcher -- precise time control @Test fun testDebounce() = runTest { val dispatcher = StandardTestDispatcher(testScheduler) val viewModel = SearchViewModel(FakeRepo(), dispatcher) viewModel.onQueryChanged("kot") advanceTimeBy(100) // only 100ms -- not enough for 300ms debounce assertTrue(viewModel.results.value.isEmpty()) // nothing yet viewModel.onQueryChanged("kotlin") // new input resets debounce timer advanceTimeBy(299) assertTrue(viewModel.results.value.isEmpty()) // still waiting advanceTimeBy(1) // now 300ms since last input assertTrue(viewModel.results.value.isNotEmpty()) // debounce fired! } // Testing retry with exponential backoff @Test fun retryWithBackoff() = runTest { val fakeApi = FakeApi() fakeApi.failCount = 2 // fail first 2 attempts val repo = RetryingRepository(fakeApi) val result = async { repo.fetchData() } advanceTimeBy(1_000) // 1st retry after 1s delay assertFalse(result.isCompleted) advanceTimeBy(2_000) // 2nd retry after 2s delay (exponential) assertFalse(result.isCompleted) advanceTimeBy(4_000) // 3rd attempt succeeds after 4s delay advanceUntilIdle() assertEquals("data", result.await()) }

Testing Timeout and Cancellation

// Test that a timeout works correctly @Test fun fetchWithTimeout_throwsOnSlowResponse() = runTest { val slowApi = FakeApi().apply { responseDelay = 10_000L } val repo = UserRepository(slowApi, timeout = 5_000L) assertThrows<TimeoutCancellationException> { repo.fetchUser("1") } } // Test that cancellation cleans up properly @Test fun cancelledScope_stopsWork() = runTest { var cleanedUp = false val job = launch { try { delay(Long.MAX_VALUE) // simulate long-running work } finally { cleanedUp = true // cleanup in finally block } } advanceTimeBy(100) job.cancel() advanceUntilIdle() assertTrue(cleanedUp) }
Key rule for TestDispatchers: runTest uses StandardTestDispatcher by default. If your coroutines aren't running, you probably need advanceUntilIdle(). If you want coroutines to run immediately without advancing, create an UnconfinedTestDispatcher() and pass it to runTest(UnconfinedTestDispatcher()) or use it with Dispatchers.setMain().

Injecting Dispatchers for Testability

// ANTI-PATTERN: hardcoded dispatcher -- untestable class BadRepository { suspend fun fetchData() = withContext(Dispatchers.IO) { // hardcoded! api.getData() } } // CORRECT: inject dispatcher -- testable class GoodRepository( private val api: Api, private val ioDispatcher: CoroutineDispatcher = Dispatchers.IO ) { suspend fun fetchData() = withContext(ioDispatcher) { api.getData() } } // In tests, inject the test dispatcher @Test fun fetchData_returnsMappedData() = runTest { val repo = GoodRepository( api = FakeApi(), ioDispatcher = StandardTestDispatcher(testScheduler) ) val result = repo.fetchData() assertEquals(expected, result) }

Repository Testing

Repositories sit between ViewModels and data sources (Room, Retrofit, DataStore). They coordinate caching, mapping, and error handling. Test them with fakes for speed, or with in-memory Room and MockWebServer for integration-level confidence.

Fakes vs Real Components for Repository Tests

Repository Testing Strategies: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Strategy Speed What It Catches Where ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Fake data sources ~ms Mapping/logic bugs test/ In-memory Room ~100ms SQL query bugs test/ (Robolectric) MockWebServer ~100ms Serialization bugs test/ (Robolectric) Room + MockWebServer ~200ms Full integration test/ (Robolectric) ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Use fakes for most tests. Use real components for integration tests.

In-Memory Room Database Testing

// Test with in-memory Room database // Runs on JVM with Robolectric -- no emulator needed @RunWith(RobolectricTestRunner::class) class ArticleRepositoryTest { private lateinit var db: AppDatabase private lateinit var dao: ArticleDao @Before fun setup() { // inMemoryDatabaseBuilder creates a DB that lives only in RAM // It's destroyed when the test ends -- no cleanup needed db = Room.inMemoryDatabaseBuilder( ApplicationProvider.getApplicationContext(), AppDatabase::class.java ).allowMainThreadQueries() // OK in tests, never in production! .build() dao = db.articleDao() } @After fun tearDown() { db.close() } @Test fun insertAndQuery() = runTest { val article = Article(1, "Testing Room", "Body text") dao.insert(article) val result = dao.getById(1) assertEquals(article, result) } @Test fun getAll_returnsInsertionOrder() = runTest { dao.insert(Article(1, "First", "a")) dao.insert(Article(2, "Second", "b")) val all = dao.getAll() assertEquals(2, all.size) assertEquals("First", all[0].title) } @Test fun delete_removesArticle() = runTest { dao.insert(Article(1, "Test", "body")) dao.deleteById(1) assertNull(dao.getById(1)) } // Testing Room's Flow-based reactive queries @Test fun observeAll_emitsOnChange() = runTest { dao.observeAll().test { assertEquals(emptyList<Article>(), awaitItem()) dao.insert(Article(1, "New", "body")) val updated = awaitItem() assertEquals(1, updated.size) cancelAndConsumeRemainingEvents() } } }

MockWebServer for API Testing

// Test with MockWebServer (OkHttp) -- a local HTTP server for tests class UserApiTest { private val mockServer = MockWebServer() private lateinit var api: UserApi @Before fun setup() { mockServer.start() // Point Retrofit at the local mock server api = Retrofit.Builder() .baseUrl(mockServer.url("/")) .addConverterFactory(Json.asConverterFactory("application/json".toMediaType())) .build() .create(UserApi::class.java) } @After fun tearDown() { mockServer.shutdown() } @Test fun getUser_parsesResponseCorrectly() = runTest { // Enqueue a canned response mockServer.enqueue( MockResponse() .setBody("""{ "id": "1", "name": "Alice", "email": "alice@test.com" }""") .setResponseCode(200) .addHeader("Content-Type", "application/json") ) val user = api.getUser("1") assertEquals("Alice", user.name) assertEquals("alice@test.com", user.email) // Verify the request was correctly formatted val request = mockServer.takeRequest() assertEquals("/users/1", request.path) assertEquals("GET", request.method) } @Test fun getUser_handles404() = runTest { mockServer.enqueue(MockResponse().setResponseCode(404)) assertThrows<HttpException> { api.getUser("999") } } @Test fun getUser_handlesNetworkTimeout() = runTest { // Simulate a slow response mockServer.enqueue( MockResponse() .setBody("{}") .setBodyDelay(10, TimeUnit.SECONDS) ) assertThrows<SocketTimeoutException> { api.getUser("1") } } @Test fun getUser_handlesMalformedJson() = runTest { mockServer.enqueue( MockResponse() .setBody("not json at all") .setResponseCode(200) ) assertThrows<SerializationException> { api.getUser("1") } } } // Integration: Repository + MockWebServer + In-Memory Room class UserRepositoryIntegrationTest { private val mockServer = MockWebServer() private lateinit var db: AppDatabase private lateinit var repo: UserRepository @Before fun setup() { mockServer.start() db = Room.inMemoryDatabaseBuilder( ApplicationProvider.getApplicationContext(), AppDatabase::class.java ).allowMainThreadQueries().build() val api = Retrofit.Builder() .baseUrl(mockServer.url("/")) .addConverterFactory(Json.asConverterFactory("application/json".toMediaType())) .build().create(UserApi::class.java) repo = UserRepositoryImpl(api, db.userDao()) } @Test fun fetchUser_cachesInRoom() = runTest { mockServer.enqueue(MockResponse().setBody("""{ "id": "1", "name": "Alice" }""")) // First call hits network and caches val user = repo.getUser("1") assertEquals("Alice", user?.name) // Verify it was cached in Room val cached = db.userDao().getById("1") assertEquals("Alice", cached?.name) } }

UI Testing

Android has two main UI testing frameworks: Compose Test for Jetpack Compose UIs and Espresso for View-based UIs. Both follow the same pattern: find a UI element, perform an action, assert a result.

Compose Test APIs

// Compose UI testing -- uses a semantic tree instead of View hierarchy class LoginScreenTest { @get:Rule val composeTestRule = createComposeRule() @Test fun loginFlow_showsErrorOnInvalidEmail() { composeTestRule.setContent { LoginScreen(onLogin = {}) } // FIND: by testTag, text, or content description composeTestRule .onNodeWithTag("email_field") .performTextInput("not-an-email") // ACT: click, type, scroll, swipe composeTestRule .onNodeWithText("Login") .performClick() // ASSERT: isDisplayed, exists, isEnabled, hasText composeTestRule .onNodeWithText("Invalid email") .assertIsDisplayed() } @Test fun loginFlow_successNavigates() { var navigated = false composeTestRule.setContent { LoginScreen(onLogin = { navigated = true }) } composeTestRule.onNodeWithTag("email_field").performTextInput("alice@test.com") composeTestRule.onNodeWithTag("password_field").performTextInput("password123") composeTestRule.onNodeWithText("Login").performClick() assertTrue(navigated) } // Testing lazy lists (LazyColumn, LazyRow) @Test fun userList_scrollsToItem() { composeTestRule.setContent { UserListScreen(users = List(100) { User("$it", "User $it") }) } // Scroll to an item that's off-screen composeTestRule.onNodeWithTag("user_list") .performScrollToIndex(50) composeTestRule.onNodeWithText("User 50") .assertIsDisplayed() } // Testing with semantics matchers @Test fun errorState_showsRetryButton() { composeTestRule.setContent { ErrorScreen(message = "Network error", onRetry = {}) } composeTestRule.onNode( hasText("Network error") and hasAnyAncestor(hasTestTag("error_container")) ).assertIsDisplayed() composeTestRule.onNode( hasText("Retry") and hasClickAction() ).assertIsEnabled() } }
Compose Test Cheat Sheet:
Find: onNodeWithText(), onNodeWithTag(), onNodeWithContentDescription(), onNode(matcher)
Act: performClick(), performTextInput(), performScrollToIndex(), performTouchInput { swipeUp() }
Assert: assertIsDisplayed(), assertExists(), assertIsEnabled(), assertTextEquals(), assertHasClickAction()
Combine matchers: hasText("x") and hasClickAction(), hasTestTag("y") or hasContentDescription("z")

Espresso (View-based UI testing)

// Espresso -- for View-based UIs (XML layouts, Fragments) // Pattern: onView(MATCHER).perform(ACTION).check(ASSERTION) @Test fun loginButton_isDisplayed() { // FIND by ID, text, content description, or custom matcher onView(withId(R.id.btn_login)) .check(matches(isDisplayed())) // ACT: type text, click, scroll, swipe onView(withId(R.id.et_email)) .perform(typeText("alice@test.com"), closeSoftKeyboard()) onView(withId(R.id.btn_login)) .perform(click()) // ASSERT: check visibility, text, enabled state onView(withText("Welcome")) .check(matches(isDisplayed())) } // Testing RecyclerView items @Test fun recyclerView_showsItems() { onView(withId(R.id.recycler_view)) .perform(RecyclerViewActions.scrollToPosition<ViewHolder>(10)) onView(withText("Item 10")) .check(matches(isDisplayed())) } // Espresso Intents -- verifying navigation @Test fun clickProfile_opensProfileActivity() { Intents.init() onView(withId(R.id.btn_profile)).perform(click()) intended(hasComponent(ProfileActivity::class.java.name)) Intents.release() }

Compose + Espresso Interop

// If your app mixes Compose and Views, you can use both in one test @Test fun hybridScreen_test() { // Espresso for the View-based toolbar onView(withId(R.id.toolbar_title)) .check(matches(withText("Home"))) // Compose test rule for the Compose content area composeTestRule.onNodeWithText("Welcome") .assertIsDisplayed() }

Flow Testing with Turbine

Turbine (by Cash App) is the standard library for testing Kotlin Flows. It provides the .test { } extension that collects emissions and lets you assert them one at a time, with proper timeout handling and error detection.

Core Turbine APIs

Turbine API Quick Reference: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ awaitItem() Wait for next emission awaitError() Wait for an error (Flow threw) awaitComplete() Wait for Flow completion cancelAndConsumeRemainingEvents() Cancel and drain cancelAndIgnoreRemainingEvents() Cancel without checking skipItems(count) Skip N emissions expectNoEvents() Assert nothing was emitted ensureAllEventsConsumed() Assert all events were handled ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Default timeout: 3 seconds per await. Configurable.
// Basic Flow testing @Test fun searchFlow_emitsResultsAfterDebounce() = runTest { val viewModel = SearchViewModel(FakeSearchRepo()) viewModel.results.test { // Rapid-fire inputs -- only last one should produce results viewModel.onQueryChanged("kot") viewModel.onQueryChanged("kotl") viewModel.onQueryChanged("kotlin") advanceTimeBy(500) // wait for debounce val emission = awaitItem() assertEquals(listOf("Kotlin", "Kotlin Coroutines"), emission) cancelAndConsumeRemainingEvents() } } // Testing error flows @Test fun errorFlow_emitsException() = runTest { val errorFlow = flow<String> { throw IOException("network fail") } errorFlow.test { val error = awaitError() assertTrue(error is IOException) assertEquals("network fail", error.message) } } // Testing StateFlow state transitions @Test fun viewModel_stateTransitions() = runTest { val viewModel = UserViewModel(FakeRepo()) viewModel.uiState.test { assertEquals(UiState.Initial, awaitItem()) // initial viewModel.loadData() assertEquals(UiState.Loading, awaitItem()) // loading assertEquals(UiState.Success(data), awaitItem()) // success cancelAndConsumeRemainingEvents() } } // Testing Flow completion @Test fun finiteFlow_completes() = runTest { val flow = flowOf(1, 2, 3) flow.test { assertEquals(1, awaitItem()) assertEquals(2, awaitItem()) assertEquals(3, awaitItem()) awaitComplete() // Flow finished } } // Skip intermediate states you don't care about @Test fun viewModel_skipToFinalState() = runTest { val viewModel = UserViewModel(FakeRepo()) viewModel.uiState.test { skipItems(2) // skip Initial and Loading val final = awaitItem() assertTrue(final is UiState.Success) cancelAndConsumeRemainingEvents() } }
Turbine gotcha with StateFlow: StateFlow always replays the current value to new collectors. So the first awaitItem() in .test { } will be the current value, not the next emission. Plan your assertions accordingly -- the initial state comes first.

Screenshot Testing

Screenshot tests catch visual regressions by comparing rendered UI against saved "golden" images pixel-by-pixel. They answer: "Does my composable still look the same after my change?" This is invaluable for design systems, component libraries, and theme changes.

Tool Comparison

ToolRuns onSpeedCompose SupportSetup
RoborazziJVM (Robolectric)Fast -- no emulatorFullGradle plugin + Robolectric
PaparazziJVM (layoutlib)Fast -- no emulatorFullSquare's Gradle plugin
Compose Preview ScreenshotJVMFastFullGoogle's official AGP tool
Screenshot Testing Workflow: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 1. RECORD (first run): ./gradlew recordRoborazzi Creates golden images in src/test/screenshots/ 2. VERIFY (CI/subsequent runs): ./gradlew verifyRoborazzi Renders component, compares pixel-by-pixel to golden PASSES if difference < threshold (e.g., 0.1%) FAILS if visual change detected 3. UPDATE (intentional change): ./gradlew recordRoborazzi Regenerates goldens, commit new images to VCS Same workflow for Paparazzi: recordPaparazzi / verifyPaparazzi ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Roborazzi Example

// build.gradle.kts setup plugins { id("io.github.takahirom.roborazzi") version "1.26.0" } // Roborazzi screenshot test @RunWith(RobolectricTestRunner::class) @GraphicsMode(GraphicsMode.Mode.NATIVE) // required for Robolectric rendering class ProfileScreenTest { @get:Rule val composeTestRule = createComposeRule() @Test fun profileScreen_default() { composeTestRule.setContent { AppTheme { ProfileScreen(user = User("1", "Alice", "alice@test.com")) } } // captureRoboImage saves/compares against golden image composeTestRule.onRoot().captureRoboImage( filePath = "src/test/screenshots/ProfileScreen_default.png" ) } // Test multiple states: empty, loading, error, dark theme @Test fun profileScreen_loading() { composeTestRule.setContent { AppTheme { ProfileScreen(state = UiState.Loading) } } composeTestRule.onRoot().captureRoboImage( filePath = "src/test/screenshots/ProfileScreen_loading.png" ) } @Test fun profileScreen_darkTheme() { composeTestRule.setContent { AppTheme(darkTheme = true) { ProfileScreen(user = User("1", "Alice", "alice@test.com")) } } composeTestRule.onRoot().captureRoboImage( filePath = "src/test/screenshots/ProfileScreen_dark.png" ) } }

Paparazzi Example

// Paparazzi -- Square's screenshot testing library // Uses Android's layoutlib directly (same renderer as Android Studio preview) class ButtonScreenshotTest { @get:Rule val paparazzi = Paparazzi( deviceConfig = DeviceConfig.PIXEL_5, theme = "android:Theme.Material3.Light", renderingMode = SessionParams.RenderingMode.SHRINK // fit to content ) @Test fun primaryButton_default() { paparazzi.snapshot { PrimaryButton(text = "Submit", onClick = {}) } } @Test fun primaryButton_disabled() { paparazzi.snapshot { PrimaryButton(text = "Submit", onClick = {}, enabled = false) } } // Test multiple device configurations @Test fun card_tablet() { paparazzi.unsafeUpdateConfig( DeviceConfig.NEXUS_10 // tablet ) paparazzi.snapshot { UserCard(user = testUser) } } } // Record goldens: ./gradlew recordPaparazziDebug // Verify goldens: ./gradlew verifyPaparazziDebug
When to use which tool: Roborazzi if you already use Robolectric and want minimal setup. Paparazzi if you want the most accurate rendering (uses Android Studio's renderer). Compose Preview Screenshot if you want to reuse @Preview functions as test inputs (Google's official solution, integrated with AGP). All three run on JVM -- no emulator needed, fast CI.
Screenshot test gotchas: Golden images are platform-dependent (macOS vs Linux render fonts slightly differently). Pin your CI to a specific OS/JDK version, or use a tolerance threshold. Also commit golden images to version control so reviewers can see visual diffs in PRs.

Robolectric vs Instrumented Tests

Every Android test faces this choice: run on the JVM (fast, simulated) or on a device/emulator (slow, real). Understanding when to use each is critical for a fast, reliable test suite.

Robolectric (JVM)Instrumented (Device/Emulator)
SpeedMilliseconds to secondsSeconds to minutes
Wheresrc/test/src/androidTest/
FrameworkSimulated Android (shadows)Real Android framework
Fidelity~95% -- may have gaps100% -- real device behavior
Best forViewModel, Repository, Utils, simple UI, screenshot testsHardware (camera, sensors), complex gestures, multi-process, real rendering
CINo emulator neededRequires emulator/device farm
Decision rule: Start with Robolectric. Only move to instrumented tests if: (1) you're testing real hardware interaction, (2) you've found a Robolectric fidelity gap that matters, or (3) you need pixel-perfect rendering validation on a real device. Most apps can cover 90%+ of tests on JVM.

Testing with Hilt

Hilt (Dagger's Android wrapper) provides built-in testing support to swap production modules with test modules, inject fakes, and test components in isolation.

// Replace the real module with a fake for testing @HiltAndroidTest @UninstallModules(RepositoryModule::class) // remove prod module class SearchScreenTest { @get:Rule(order = 0) val hiltRule = HiltAndroidRule(this) @get:Rule(order = 1) val composeTestRule = createComposeRule() // @BindValue injects this fake directly into the Hilt graph // No need to create a separate test module @BindValue val fakeRepo: UserRepository = FakeUserRepository() @Before fun setup() { hiltRule.inject() // trigger Hilt injection } @Test fun showsResults() { (fakeRepo as FakeUserRepository).seedUsers(User("1", "Alice")) composeTestRule.setContent { SearchScreen() } composeTestRule.onNodeWithText("Alice").assertExists() } } // Alternative: create a test module instead of @BindValue @Module @InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class) object TestRepositoryModule { @Provides fun provideUserRepository(): UserRepository = FakeUserRepository() }
Hilt test tip: Use @BindValue for simple replacements (one field = one binding). Use a test @Module when you need complex setup, scoping, or multiple bindings. Always specify order on rules: HiltAndroidRule must come before ComposeTestRule.

Testing Best Practices Summary

Android Testing Checklist: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 1. Follow the pyramid: 70% unit, 20% integration, 10% UI 2. Prefer fakes over mocks (test behavior, not implementation) 3. Inject dispatchers -- never hardcode Dispatchers.IO/Main 4. Use runTest for all coroutine tests 5. Use Turbine for Flow/StateFlow assertions 6. Use in-memory Room for DAO tests 7. Use MockWebServer for API tests 8. Add screenshot tests for design system components 9. Keep tests fast: prefer Robolectric over instrumented 10. Test edge cases: errors, empty states, loading, cancellation ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
// Dependencies you need (build.gradle.kts) testImplementation("junit:junit:4.13.2") testImplementation("org.jetbrains.kotlinx:kotlinx-coroutines-test:1.8.1") testImplementation("app.cash.turbine:turbine:1.1.0") testImplementation("androidx.test:core-ktx:1.6.1") testImplementation("androidx.room:room-testing:2.6.1") testImplementation("com.squareup.okhttp3:mockwebserver:4.12.0") testImplementation("io.mockk:mockk:1.13.10") testImplementation("org.robolectric:robolectric:4.12.2") testImplementation("io.github.takahirom.roborazzi:roborazzi:1.26.0") testImplementation("com.google.dagger:hilt-android-testing:2.51") // Compose testing testImplementation("androidx.compose.ui:ui-test-junit4") debugImplementation("androidx.compose.ui:ui-test-manifest") // Instrumented tests androidTestImplementation("androidx.test.espresso:espresso-core:3.6.1") androidTestImplementation("androidx.test.ext:junit:1.2.1")

questions.title: Compose UI Test
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to write a Compose UI test.
questions.explanation: createComposeRule() creates a test environment. onNodeWithText() finds by text. assertExists() verifies presence. onNode(hasText()) uses matchers. performClick() simulates user input.
hints: The test rule creates a Compose testing environment

questions.title: Testing Coroutines
questions.description: This ViewModel test is flaky — sometimes passes, sometimes fails. Fix the coroutine testing setup.

checks.message: Use runTest and set up a TestDispatcher

questions.explanation: Coroutines in viewModelScope use Dispatchers.Main, which doesn't exist in unit tests. Fix: Dispatchers.setMain(testDispatcher) replaces Main. runTest properly advances coroutine execution. Always reset in @After.
hints: viewModelScope uses Dispatchers.Main — does that exist in a JVM test?

questions.title: Test Doubles
questions.description: Which test double provides a simplified but working implementation?
options: Mock — records interactions
Stub — returns hardcoded values
Fake — has a working implementation (e.g., in-memory database)
Spy — wraps a real object
questions.explanation: A Fake is a simplified but functional implementation. Example: FakeUserRepository using a HashMap instead of a real database. Google recommends fakes over mocks for most Android testing.
hints: Which test double actually has working logic?

questions.title: ViewModel Unit Test
questions.description: Write a unit test for a ViewModel that tests the loading state flow: initial Loading → Success with data.
questions.explanation: Key: test dispatcher replaces Dispatchers.Main, fake repo provides data, runTest handles coroutines, assert against StateFlow value.
hints: You need three things: test dispatcher, fake repository, and runTest

questions.title: Turbine Flow Testing
questions.description: What does this test verify using the Turbine library?
options: It verifies that every keystroke triggers a search
It verifies that the search debounces — only the last query ("kotlin") produces results after 500ms
It verifies that the search filters out short queries
It verifies that search results are cached
questions.explanation: Turbine is a testing library for Kotlin Flows. .test { } collects emissions. Three queries are sent rapidly, then advanceTimeBy(500) simulates the debounce delay. Only one emission is expected — the results for "kotlin". This confirms the debounce works: intermediate queries are ignored, only the final one triggers a search after the delay.
hints: advanceTimeBy simulates time passing — why is that needed for debounce?
Only one awaitItem() is called — meaning only one emission is expected

questions.title: Fake Repository
questions.description: Write a FakeUserRepository that implements UserRepository interface. It should store users in a mutable list and support getAll(), insert(), and clear() operations.

checks.message: Store users in an in-memory list

questions.explanation: A fake uses real data structures (in-memory list) instead of a real database. toList() returns a defensive copy. Extra methods like setUsers() and clear() make test setup easy. This is simpler and more reliable than mocking.
hints: Use a MutableList as the backing store
Add helper methods for test setup convenience

questions.title: Testing Pyramid
questions.description: According to the testing pyramid, which type of test should make up the majority of your test suite?
options: UI / end-to-end tests — they give the most confidence
Integration tests — they balance speed and confidence
Unit tests — they are fast, isolated, and deterministic
Manual tests — automated tests can't catch everything
questions.explanation: The testing pyramid recommends ~70% unit tests, ~20% integration, ~10% UI tests. Unit tests are fast (milliseconds), run on JVM, and test logic in isolation. They're cheap to write and maintain. UI tests give high confidence but are slow and flaky. A healthy test suite has many fast unit tests and few targeted UI tests for critical flows.
hints: Which test type is fastest and cheapest to maintain?

questions.title: Screenshot Testing Tools
questions.description: Which screenshot testing tool runs entirely on the JVM (no emulator needed) and uses Robolectric's native rendering?
options: Espresso Screenshot
Roborazzi — JVM-based screenshot capture using Robolectric
Firebase Test Lab — cloud device screenshots
Layout Inspector — manual visual comparison
questions.explanation: Roborazzi captures screenshots on the JVM using Robolectric's rendering engine. No emulator or device needed — tests run in seconds. It compares against golden images and fails if there are visual differences beyond a threshold. Paparazzi (by Square) is similar but uses Android's layoutlib directly. Both are fast CI-friendly alternatives to device-based screenshot tests.
hints: The fastest screenshot tests don't need a device at all

questions.title: Robolectric vs Instrumented
questions.description: When should you prefer instrumented tests over Robolectric?
options: Always — Robolectric is outdated and unreliable
When you need 100% fidelity with real Android behavior, such as testing hardware sensors, complex animations, or multi-process scenarios
For all ViewModel tests to ensure accuracy
When testing pure Kotlin logic with no Android dependencies
questions.explanation: Robolectric simulates the Android framework on JVM — it's fast but may have fidelity gaps for advanced features. Use instrumented tests when you need the real Android framework: hardware sensors, camera, complex touch gestures, multi-process IPC, or behavior that differs across API levels. For ViewModels, Repositories, and simple UI, Robolectric is faster and sufficient.
hints: Robolectric simulates Android — some edge cases behave differently

questions.title: Hilt Test Setup
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to set up a Hilt-enabled test that replaces the real repository with a fake.

blanks.hint: Marks a test class for Hilt injection
Removes the real module so you can replace it
Binds this field directly into the Hilt graph

questions.explanation: @HiltAndroidTest enables Hilt in tests. @UninstallModules removes the production module that provides UserRepository. @BindValue injects the fake directly into the graph — no module needed. hiltRule.inject() triggers injection. This pattern lets you test with controlled data without mocking frameworks.
hints: Three annotations set up the replacement: test marker, uninstall, and bind
@BindValue is simpler than creating a test module

questions.title: Screenshot Test
questions.description: Write a Roborazzi screenshot test that captures the UserCard composable in both light and dark themes.
questions.explanation: Screenshot tests render a composable and capture an image. On first run, it creates golden images. On subsequent runs, it compares against goldens and fails if there's a visual difference. Testing both themes catches color/contrast regressions. captureRoboImage() is Roborazzi's API — Paparazzi uses paparazzi.snapshot { }.
hints: Wrap the composable in your theme with darkTheme parameter
captureRoboImage saves/compares against a golden image file
